text:
The New York Jets' offensive line is actually in a good place right now overall.

That's a statement that would've surprised some people over the last few years, but New York's offensive line actually was a bright spot throughout the 2025 season, starting with rookie offensive tackle Armand Membou. The offensive line also was healthy for the most part. Membou, Olu Fashanu, Joe Tippmann, Josh Myers and John Simpson were together for all 17 games for New York in 2025.

If you like our content, choose Sports Illustrated as a preferred source on Google.

The line held up and the team only has one hole to address this offseason at guard. Simpson is a pending free agent. So, too, is Alijah Vera-Tucker, who missed the entire 2025 season after tearing his triceps. If Vera-Tucker is healthy, he would be the better option. This is a guy who was viewed as the most underrated player on the roster heading into the 2025 season before getting hurt. Health is the big question, though.

The Jets have a decision to make at guard

Vera-Tucker played in just 12 games total across the 2022 and 2023 seasons and then missed the 2025 campaign. That's a lot of time on the shelf. But he still is just 26 years old and has massive upside. But will he come back in free agency? FOX Sports' Greg Auman predicted that he will.

"No. 42. Alijah Vera-Tucker, G, Jets," Auman wrote. "Tucker, 26, missed all of 2025 with a torn triceps muscle, and his injury history — playing 43 of a possible 85 games in five years — makes it hard to project a market for him. Pro Football Focus had him as the league's No. 11 guard entering 2025, and even coming off the injury, they project him to a four-year deal worth $70 million. That's $17.5 million a year. It's reasonable to think he'll come in short of that, if only because of the injury problems that have ended three of his last four seasons early. Prediction: Re-signs with Jets."

If healthy, the offensive line would be better than it was in 2025 simply with Vera-Tucker in the mix. He's that good. But the health questions remain. If the Jets could bring him in on a one- or two-year, prove-it deal, that would be the ideal situation.

title: Patriots NFL mock draft: Could New England address the offensive line in Round 1?

text:
The offseason is going to move quickly for the New England Patriots, a byproduct of playing into February.

The NFL Scouting Combine is only a week and a half away. Patriots scouts will convene there for interviews with prospects. Free agency follows after that. Then the draft.

So it won’t be long until the Patriots begin to revamp a roster that reached the Super Bowl. But they have a lot of needs — and a lot of draft picks. So with the combine on the horizon, here’s our first mock draft of the season, making a selection for all 11 picks they currently own.

Round 1, pick No. 31: Kadyn Proctor, OT, Alabama

Even with the Patriots committed to keeping Will Campbell at left tackle, and even with the possibility that Morgan Moses returns (and, thus, does not retire) to play right tackle, tackle could still be a need for the Patriots after the playoff woes on the offensive line.

A pick like Proctor would give them some flexibility looking ahead. If Campbell plays great at left tackle next season, then Proctor simply becomes the right tackle. If Campbell struggles, you could slide him inside to guard while putting Proctor at left tackle. If Moses struggles or suffers an injury, Proctor could play right away on the right side.

Proctor has been a can’t-miss prospect for years. He’s 6-foot-7 with long arms and was the fifth-ranked recruit in the country coming out of high school.

Round 2: Derrick Moore, Edge, Michigan

Free agency will be telling about the Patriots’ plan at edge rusher, but they have some tough decisions to make. Harold Landry dealt with injuries down the stretch. K’Lavon Chaisson had the best season of his career, but it’s not a given that the Pats will re-sign him.

They could also look for star power here, potentially even in a trade for Maxx Crosby. But if the Patriots don’t bolster the position via a trade or free agency, Moore would be a good candidate as the kind of edge rusher who could play all three downs.

Round 3: Ja’Kobi Lane, WR, USC

Lane would give the Patriots a legitimate and big (6-foot-4) outside-the-numbers wide receiver, which would allow them to play Stefon Diggs more in the slot. Mack Hollins plays a similar role, but Lane would offer much more upside.

Lane was great in the red zone in college, which could help a Patriots team that doesn’t have a ton of options there and too often relied on explosive plays to move the ball.

Round 4: Justin Joly, TE, NC State

The Pats can’t keep ignoring the tight end position. They have to get better there and add depth, either via free agency or the draft (maybe both).

Joly doesn’t project as a great run blocker, which offensive coordinator Josh McDaniels would surely want, but he’s a good receiver who could give Drake Maye another option in the passing game.

Round 4: Aiden Fisher, LB, Indiana

While Robert Spillane dealt with injuries, the Patriots’ depth at linebacker was tested. Jack Gibbens and Christian Elliss played admirably, but it wouldn’t be a bad idea to add depth in the middle rounds of the draft.

As a bonus, linebackers taken around this time — like Fisher might be — tend to contribute early on special teams.

Round 5: Isaiah Nwokobia, S, SMU

It’ll be interesting to see whether the Patriots re-sign Jaylinn Hawkins. They struck gold with fourth-round rookie safety Craig Woodson, and if they don’t re-sign Hawkins, perhaps they could turn to another mid-round safety.

Nwokobia is an aggressive, downhill safety who would pair well with Woodson on the back end of the defense.

Round 6: Devon Marshall, CB, NC State

After an early-season injury to Christian Gonzalez, the Patriots’ cornerbacks were relatively healthy in 2025. That was a good thing because they lacked depth at the position. It would be wise to invest there again.

Marshall is undersized, but he made a good impact at NC State after playing high school football at Catholic Memorial in West Roxbury, Mass.

Round 6: Ethan Onianwa, G, Ohio State

If the Patriots want to get bigger on the offensive line while improving their depth, Onianwa could be a late-round option. He’s massive (6-foot-7) but can play on the interior, which could give the Patriots flexibility regarding what they want to do with Jared Wilson.

Round 6: Brett Thorson, P, Georgia

Punter Bryce Baringer wasn’t so bad that the Patriots have to go into this offseason set on replacing him. But with four picks in the sixth round, it wouldn’t be a bad idea to find some competition for him and try to upgrade at the position.

Round 6: Robert Henry Jr., RB, UTSA

One of the Patriots’ under-the-radar needs is depth at running back. They pretty much utilized only two all season in Rhamondre Stevenson and TreVeyon Henderson. And the lack of depth meant they were wary of using Henderson as a kick returner, because they didn’t want to risk injury to one of their backs.

So a late-round running back could give them flexibility. Henry averaged 6.9 yards per rush last season while adding a pair of touchdown receptions.

Round 7: Eli Heidenreich, FB, Navy

The Patriots hoped Brock Lampe would be their fullback this season, but he suffered a season-ending injury during training camp. Without him, they moved Jack Westover to the position even though he wasn’t a natural fit.

If McDaniels wants a fullback in his offense, they may have to draft one.

title: Why Lamar wasn't himself in 2025? Past NFL MVPs weigh in - ESPN
authors: Jamison Hensley, Field Yates, Jeremy Fowler, Ben Solak, John Keim, Sarah Barshop, Jeff Legwold, Courtney Cronin, Eric Woodyard, Alaina Getzenberg
description:
In the Baltimore Ravens' season finale in Pittsburgh, Lamar Jackson looked like his two-time NFL Most Valuable Player self again, putting together his best quarter in what had been a frustrating year.
But last season, Jackson struggled with his accuracy, consistency and explosiveness.
This past season, Jackson had the worst pass protection of his career.
After the season, Ravens general manager Eric DeCosta pointed out that the offensive line has to be more consistent and "didn't gel quite as much this year."
The most drastic change in Jackson's game in 2025 was the deep decline in rushing attempts.
text:
In the Baltimore Ravens' season finale in Pittsburgh, Lamar Jackson looked like his two-time NFL Most Valuable Player self again, putting together his best quarter in what had been a frustrating year.

Jackson launched touchdown passes of 50 and 64 yards to Zay Flowers and even turned a near-certain sack into a magical escape. This marked the second time in NFL history that a player threw two go-ahead touchdowns of 50 yards or longer in the fourth quarter, according to ESPN Research.

It was a performance that would have carried Baltimore to an AFC North title, if not for Tyler Loop's missed 44-yard field goal as time expired. Instead, Baltimore's 26-24 loss to Pittsburgh led to an offseason filled with questions, starting with a difficult one: Where was this Jackson all season?

"I don't think it's any one thing when you look at him," said Rich Gannon, a former NFL quarterback who is a host on Sirius NFL Radio.

Three past NFL MVP QBs -- Gannon, along with Matt Ryan and Kurt Warner -- weighed in on the issues that impacted Jackson's tumultuous season, from his reduced practice time to poor pass protection and a decline in rushing attempts.

The biggest ramification of the Ravens' 8-9 season was the surprise firing of John Harbaugh, who was the franchise's winningest coach after 18 seasons. Now, for new coach Jesse Minter, the priority is to get Jackson back on track. Minter recently told the Ravens' website that he wants an offensive system where Jackson doesn't have to feel like he's "Superman" every time the ball is snapped.

Jackson is one of the NFL's all-time unique talents due to his ability to win games with his arm and elusiveness. He is the league's career leader in passer rating (102.2) and rushing yards by a quarterback (6,522).

But last season, Jackson struggled with his accuracy, consistency and explosiveness. With a 6-7 record, which became his first losing season as a starting quarterback, Jackson averaged 196.1 yards passing per game and threw 21 touchdown passes -- his fewest since 2022.

"As I was watching film, I couldn't put my finger on anything that said, 'Oh, OK, here's what he's doing different' or 'He's making bad decisions now that he didn't make before,'" said Warner, who was the NFL MVP in 1999 and 2001. "It wasn't that. It was more just missing plays that he's made a million times before."

WHEN JACKSON LEFT the Week 4 game in Kansas City with a hamstring injury, he and the Ravens' offense were never the same for the rest of the season.

In Jackson's first three starts, Baltimore was the highest-scoring team in the league, averaging 37 points per game. After Jackson returned from his hamstring injury five weeks later, the Ravens scored fewer than 30 points in each of Jackson's last 10 starts, which was the longest single-season drought of his career.

"Even coming back [from injury], I think what often gets discounted is you're available to play, but it's just hard to be sharp when you're not practicing and you're missing time during the week," said Ryan, the current president of football for the Atlanta Falcons who was the NFL MVP in 2016. "And even when you are [practicing], you're laboring to get through it. So I think that makes it a little bit more difficult."

Jackson endured the worst slump of his career in November and December, which coincided with a reduced amount of practice time. For six straight weeks, he missed the first practice of the week with a string of injuries (including knee, ankle and toe). He also missed time with an illness.

Over that span of six games, Jackson averaged 177 yards passing, throwing three touchdown passes and five interceptions. During this stretch, he didn't record a touchdown in three straight games for the first time in his career.

"He's had the ability in the past to go out and have fresh legs and do what he does, but eventually it can catch up to you," Gannon said. "Look, it's not just that he's missing practice time. It's the guys around him that are missing those opportunities with him. It's the red zone throws you're not getting. It's the two-minute shots. It's the third-down packages that you're not getting as many reps with."

This all led to the most inaccurate season of Jackson's career. His precision pass rate, which is the percentage of pass attempts that hit targets in stride, was 43.7%. In comparison, Jackson's two highest precision pass rates came in 2019 (54.9%) and 2023 (56.1%), which were his MVP seasons.

But Warner was hesitant to chalk up Jackson's struggles to fewer practice reps. He said he believes less practice time shouldn't significantly impact Jackson, who had enough familiarity with the game plan and supporting cast (10 of 11 starters returned on offense last season).

"One missed practice day, especially early in the week, is kind of like, OK, you're going to have plenty of days to make up for that and get back into the rhythm," Warner said. "So I don't think that's something that I would point to and say, 'Oh, that's the reason he's missing these throws.'"

ONE POINT WHERE Ryan, Warner and Gannon all agreed: No one can put all of the blame on Jackson. It's a challenge for any quarterback to perform at a high level when his supporting cast is faltering, especially when it's the offensive line.

Gannon has a saying that, when great quarterbacks get knocked around, they become good quarterbacks. And, when good quarterbacks get knocked around, they become average ones.

"My point is nobody's immune, and it's not just the sacks," Gannon said. "It's the hits, the hurries, the knockdowns. Eventually it can speed you up. I don't care how tough a guy you are, it can have an effect on your ability to sit in there and trust the protection."

In 2024, Jackson produced career highs of 4,172 yards passing and 41 touchdown passes when he had the best pass protection of his career. He was sacked on only 4.3% of his dropbacks (23 sacks on 538 dropbacks).

This past season, Jackson had the worst pass protection of his career. He was sacked on 9.8% of his dropbacks, and he faced a pressure rate of 37.1% (percentage of dropbacks where the quarterback was sacked, under duress or hit), which was the second-worst in the league in 2025.

"Oftentimes you have to trick yourself that regardless of what happened before, you've got to trust that people are going to do their job and people going to pick up the protections," Ryan said. "And so you're constantly fighting that. But to say it doesn't affect you, it does. And the great ones find ways to, even when the numbers are down or even when the sacks are up, they still find a way to keep their team in the game and give themselves an opportunity late in the game."

After the season, Ravens general manager Eric DeCosta pointed out that the offensive line has to be more consistent and "didn't gel quite as much this year." Baltimore has decisions to make with the offensive line, where Pro Bowl center Tyler Linderbaum is a free agent and the team likely has to upgrade at both starting guard spots.

"We can talk about the high-flying teams and the teams that throw it all over the field, but the best teams in the league continue to be the ones that win up front and the ones that prioritize, 'We're going to build it this way and that's where we're going to have our success,'" Warner said. "It still comes back to the teams that win in the trenches."

WARNER REMEMBERS AN interview with Harbaugh a number of years ago about the dialogue between him and Jackson on the sideline during games.

Jackson would often tell Harbaugh, "Hey, Coach, I'm just playing ball. I'm just doing what I do."

"That always stuck in my mind, because sometimes that's kind of the nature of some guys is just kind of like, 'I'm just reacting to what I see and I'm just playing. I think whatever I have to do, I'm just doing it. I'm not overthinking anything,'" Warner said. "And maybe that is an aspect of where it's at now after the injury is maybe he is overthinking it a little bit, maybe he is like, 'I don't want to take off and run. I don't want to get hurt or I don't want to put myself in harm's way.'"

The most drastic change in Jackson's game in 2025 was the deep decline in rushing attempts. He carried the ball only 5.2 times per game, three attempts per game fewer than any previous season.

By Jackson not running as much, it created less stress for opposing defenses and took a chunk of production away from the Baltimore offense. Prior to last season, Jackson averaged 59.9 rushing yards per game. In 2025, he totaled less than half of that per game (26.8).

"As you age, as you get older, you realize your availability and being able to go week in and week out is almost just as important as your ability to take off and go," Ryan said. "So I think finding that balance for the remainder of his career, that's going to be what they have to figure out. But I just don't think stripping him of that, because I think that I've often said, I thought when he is at his best, he's probably the most difficult player in the league to game plan for for defensive coordinators."

Ryan added, "And I do think when you take a little bit of that away, it's one less thing for a defense to account for and maybe doesn't open up some of the other things that when he's rolling like that, it does."

Former Ravens offensive coordinator Todd Monken often called Jackson's ability to run his "superpower." In Monken's first two seasons in Baltimore, he called an average of 4.4 designed runs for Jackson. Last season, there were an average of two designed runs per game.

Last season, Jackson chose to buy more time throwing the ball than taking off to run, scrambling 40 times for 213 yards. He was also not as explosive when he did run, failing to produce a run over 20 yards for the first time in his career.

"I think the natural reaction would be, 'Yeah, I think it's smart because the older he gets, the less he's going to be able to do that or the more his body's going to break down," Warner said.

WHEN JACKSON WON his first NFL MVP award in 2019, it seemed like a foregone conclusion that he would eventually get to a Super Bowl and win one. Seven years later, there are questions about whether Jackson will ever play on the game's biggest stage, especially after the Ravens' track record of postseason failures.

"He's on the fast track to the Hall of Fame without a doubt," Warner said. "But if he never plays his best in that moment and never gets to a Super Bowl or gets to a couple of them, that will be something that will always kind of be there hanging over him."

To reach the Super Bowl, Jackson's main obstacles in the AFC used to be beating the likes of Patrick Mahomes and Josh Allen. Now, it's becoming increasingly more difficult in the AFC with the emergence of Drake Maye and Bo Nix, who led the New England Patriots and Denver Broncos to the top two seeds in the conference.

"I still think he's going to have an opportunity to win a Super Bowl," Ryan said. "I just think he's that great of a player, but it's getting crowded. That has to be taken into consideration."

One of the reasons Ravens owner Steve Bisciotti fired Harbaugh was the team underperforming in the playoffs year after year. Baltimore has three postseason victories in Jackson's eight seasons.

In recent years, Jackson has watched Flowers fumble near the goal line in the 2023 AFC Championship Game and saw tight end Mark Andrews drop a 2-point conversion attempt in the 2024 divisional playoff game. But Jackson hasn't lived up to expectations either with 11 turnovers, which are the most by any player in the playoffs since 2018.

"I think there's only a handful of quarterbacks in the NFL that have the ability to take over a game at this level or put a team on their shoulders and carry them to the finish line," Gannon said. "I think of Josh Allen, I think of Patrick Mahomes, I think of Lamar Jackson. There's only a handful, and he's one of them."

title: NFL overhaul tiers: Rebuild time for 18 non-playoff teams - ESPN
authors: Jeremy Fowler, Eric Woodyard, Alaina Getzenberg, John Keim, Elizabeth Merrill, Bill Barnwell, Rich Cimini, Ben Solak, Field Yates, ESPN NFL experts
description:
Note: Teams are organized alphabetically within each tier, cap space is via Roster Management System, and projected draft picks do not include potential compensatory picks.
Sneaky good trajectory Atlanta Falcons 2026 cap space: minus-$12.7 million Top free agent: Kyle Pitts Sr.
Projected 2026 draft picks: 5 The hope for a quick turnaround: Kevin Stefanski's wide-zone running scheme with Bijan Robinson and a capable offensive line should be dangerous.
Moving on from free agent veterans such as Cam Jordan and Davis seems timely, even though they have held up well.
Peters has just two draft picks in the first four rounds this year because of previous trades.
text:
Now that the 2025 NFL season is over, we're looking ahead at just how much work teams that were left out of the playoffs must do this offseason -- and potentially beyond. Which teams need to tear down and regroup? Which should take a new approach to move toward contender status? Which can be in the mix after only a savvy move or two? And which are already trending in the right direction and could contend sooner rather than later?

We tiered the 18 teams that failed to make the playoffs based on their current trajectory, from no-doubt bounce-back squads to full rebuilds. And for each club, we took a closer look at the franchise's current state -- and what needs to be done in the coming months.

We start with three teams that were the toast of the NFL not long ago. Note: Teams are organized alphabetically within each tier, cap space is via Roster Management System, and projected draft picks do not include potential compensatory picks.

Jump to a team:

ARI | ATL | BAL | CIN | CLE | DAL

DET | IND | KC | LV | MIA | MIN

NO | NYG | NYJ | TB | TEN | WSH

No need to panic

Baltimore Ravens

2026 cap space: $7 million

Top free agent: Tyler Linderbaum

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: The Ravens have a chance in every game with quarterback Lamar Jackson and running back Derrick Henry leading their offense. New head coach Jesse Minter should instantly improve a much-maligned defense. Up to four more draft selections are on the way because of the compensatory pick formula.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The offensive line regressed in 2025 and needs retooling. Baltimore needs a classic outside receiver in his prime instead of the older veterans it has signed at the position. The team also lacks an elite pass rusher.

Detroit Lions

2026 cap space: minus-$23.1 million

Top free agent: Al-Quadin Muhammad

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: Detroit still has one of the league's most talented rosters and should jump right back into contention. The hiring of offensive coordinator Drew Petzing isn't splashy but will prove vital.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Detroit has committed nearly $1 billion to star players on extensions, and running back Jahmyr Gibbs is the next to be paid. Will that affect roster depth? GM Brad Holmes faces a balancing act, as the Lions need to improve their run defense and add talent to the secondary.

Kansas City Chiefs

2026 cap space: minus-$61.7 million

Top free agents: Jaylen Watson, Bryan Cook

Projected 2026 draft picks: 5

The hope for a quick turnaround: The Chiefs ran out of gas in 2025 but have most of what they need to make another run. Coach Andy Reid and quarterback Patrick Mahomes enter Year 9 together. Health has been an issue for years, but the offensive line is still quality and the team's track record in the draft is better than most. R-E-L-A-X.

Why it might take longer than hoped: A small talent drain is inevitable, especially on defense. Three defensive free agents -- Watson, Cook and Leo Chenal -- will be tough to re-sign, given Kansas City's cap situation. Chris Jones turns 32 in July. Travis Kelce turns 37 in October. The offense needs a true field-stretching boundary receiver and running back help.

A few moves away

Cincinnati Bengals

2026 cap space: $59.5 million

Top free agent: Trey Hendrickson

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: The offense is good enough to keep Cincinnati in just about every game. The window remains open to win now with Joe Burrow, Ja'Marr Chase and a strong supporting cast. The team has ample cap space to strengthen its defense via free agency.

Why it might take longer than hoped: That defense lacks high-end talent and playmaking and is about to lose its best player (Hendrickson). A unit that was supposed to improve finished 31st in total defense. Eventually, bad defense will become the identity and not an aberration unless addressed in a major way.

Dallas Cowboys

2026 cap space: minus-$66.8 million

Top free agent: George Pickens

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: The offense was humming in 2025, and the core players will return, with the team expected to franchise-tag receiver Pickens. Quarterback Dak Prescott is squarely in his prime under coach Brian Schottenheimer. The defensive line is stocked with talent, and Dallas has two first-round picks with which to address its defense because of the Micah Parsons trade.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Last season's defensive back seven underperformed, forcing a look in the mirror this offseason. The addition of defensive coordinator Christian Parker will help, but reinforcements are sorely needed. Bringing back Pickens and running back Javonte Williams would be prudent.

Indianapolis Colts

2026 cap space: $36.3 million

Top free agent: Daniel Jones

Projected 2026 draft picks: 5

The hope for a quick turnaround: The Colts were 8-2 when fully healthy. QB Daniel Jones' injury devastated the team. Coach Shane Steichen has a track record as an offensive head coach. Indy is stout at the skill positions and offensive line. DeForest Buckner and Sauce Gardner should be healthier this season and boost the defense.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Indy is going on 12 years without a division crown. There's no guarantee that changes, especially in a much-improved AFC South. The Colts lack a first-round pick because of the Gardner trade, and the defense could lose Nick Cross and Kwity Paye. It seems like the right time to take a swing on a free agent pass rusher. Alec Pierce would be a big loss if he signs elsewhere.

Minnesota Vikings

2026 cap space: minus-$59.1 million

Top free agent: Jalen Nailor

Projected 2026 draft picks: 10

The hope for a quick turnaround: Kevin O'Connell's 43-27 record through four seasons suggests the Vikings will be in the thick of the NFC North race yet again. The defense is among the league's most physical and opportunistic. The offense is still full of playmakers, and the offensive line should be healthier.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The quarterback position remaining a huge question entering the fifth season of this regime is concerning. It looks like Minnesota will add a veteran to compete with J.J. McCarthy. Several benched starters have resurrected their careers recently -- including Sam Darnold with this franchise -- and Minnesota offers a stable environment. But this is the one area holding the team back from major progress. That and an offensive line that couldn't stay healthy.

Tampa Bay Buccaneers

2026 cap space: $27.4 million

Top free agent: Mike Evans

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: Tampa Bay can score with anybody when healthy. Its receivers room is among the league's deepest, and the offensive line has a clear identity. Baker Mayfield, 30, has shown he's a quality NFL starter with whom Tampa Bay can win.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Tampa Bay falls somewhere between contention and the need for a mini rebuild. The Bucs need to address their defense this offseason. The group needs talent improvements at all three levels, most notably pass rusher This would be as good an offseason as any to take some chances to acquire quality defensive players.

Sneaky good trajectory

Atlanta Falcons

2026 cap space: minus-$12.7 million

Top free agent: Kyle Pitts Sr.

Projected 2026 draft picks: 5

The hope for a quick turnaround: Kevin Stefanski's wide-zone running scheme with Bijan Robinson and a capable offensive line should be dangerous. Atlanta was considered one of the top jobs available this cycle because of its skill-position talent. The defense has continuity under coordinator Jeff Ulbrich and major talent on the back end.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The team doesn't have a first-round pick because it traded for the right to select James Pearce Jr. in last year's draft. Pearce's future with the team is murky after last week's arrest in Florida on battery charges. The team must prioritize paying star receiver Drake London -- and finding him additional playmaking help on the outside. The team also needs at least one more cornerback and more size on the interior defensive line.

New Orleans Saints

2026 cap space: minus-$26.5 million

Top free agent: Demario Davis

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: Quarterback Tyler Shough showed promise as a starter, providing hope for the future. Wide receiver Chris Olave and left tackle Kelvin Banks Jr. are among the young pillars on offense. And the defense overachieved, ranking in the top 10 in several defensive categories. Young players in the secondary are trending well. And, look at that, the team's cap deficit isn't pushing $80 million. Progress!

Why it might take longer than hoped: The Saints' roster has been among the league's oldest for years. It's time to get younger. That's a lengthy process. Moving on from free agent veterans such as Cam Jordan and Davis seems timely, even though they have held up well. The front seven needs at least one more quality player. On offense, the backfield needs fresh legs, as Alvin Kamara turns 31 this summer.

New York Giants

2026 cap space: $21.2 million

Top free agent: Wan'Dale Robinson

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: Several head coaches experienced fast success in 2025, and John Harbaugh will try to do the same with a Year 1 turnaround in 2026. The Giants' roster is reaching respectable levels of talent after a few down years. Quarterback Jaxson Dart is a selling point. The defensive line is among the league's most talented. And receiver Malik Nabers will return from ACL and meniscus tears.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The Giants must shake their losing culture. New York has won more than six games just once in the past nine seasons. The shift might not happen overnight. The supporting casts on both sides of the ball haven't been good enough in recent years. That has to change.

Somewhere between contention and reset

Washington Commanders

2026 cap space: $51.2 million

Top free agent: Deebo Samuel

Projected 2026 draft picks: 6

The hope for a quick turnaround: Washington is a year removed from competing in the NFC title game. When healthy, Jayden Daniels is a top-10 quarterback who will move the offense into a more respectable range in 2026. GM Adam Peters has cap space to replenish the roster. Injuries plagued Washington more than most teams last season.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Washington was the hardest team of the bunch to evaluate, so it gets its own category. The Commanders were close in 2024. The roster is problematic in spots, though. Defensive impact players are older. Pass rush is a major need. And some recent high draft picks have been slow to develop. Peters has just two draft picks in the first four rounds this year because of previous trades. Adding a skill player at tight end or running back would be useful.

In the middle of a rebuild

Cleveland Browns

2026 cap space: minus-$66.2 million

Top free agent: Joel Bitonio

Projected 2026 draft picks: 10

The hope for a quick turnaround: Cleveland has an impressive defense, led by Myles Garrett but with pillars at all three levels. With four picks in the top 70 (two in the first round), the Browns can do serious work to improve an offense that already has intriguing young players such as running back Quinshon Judkins and tight end Harold Fannin Jr.

Why it might take longer than hoped: A once-dominant offensive line needs a reset. Quarterback is still an issue, with no clear-cut solutions in the draft. The team is weak at wide receiver and still paying Deshaun Watson's contract for one more year. It also needs new leadership on defense after the Jim Schwartz fiasco. Expecting a playoff appearance in 2026 is probably asking a lot.

Tennessee Titans

2026 cap space: $78.7 million

Top free agent: Chig Okonkwo

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: Quarterback Cam Ward showed enough flashes in his rookie season to create optimism for the offense's future. Midround picks Chimere Dike, Elic Ayomanor and Gunnar Helm look like hits. Head coach Robert Saleh should improve the defense, and seasoned playcaller Brian Daboll should help Ward. The offensive line is unspectacular but adequate. And Jeffery Simmons was one of the best players in football last season.

Why it might take longer than hoped: GM Mike Borgonzi enters Year 2 of a major rebuild. While the Titans need to spend at least modestly in free agency to fill out their roster, Borgonzi wants to build through the draft. That often results in a slow simmer. The lack of talent on defense is a problem.

Full-blown overhaul

Arizona Cardinals

2026 cap space: $13.5 million

Top free agent: Jalen Thompson

Projected 2026 draft picks: 7

The hope for a quick turnaround: The law of averages says Arizona will have better luck on the injury front. The team was playing third- and fourth-stringers at several spots last season. Better health will allow Arizona to run the ball more efficiently. The defense is not devoid of talent.

Why it might take longer than hoped: Arizona could be looking at a complete reset at quarterback, with Kyler Murray owed $36.8 million in guarantees in 2026 and possibly on his way out. The team lacks true blue-chip players, save for 26-year-old tight end Trey McBride and 30-year-old safety Budda Baker. The Cardinals simply weren't competitive for much of last season and must make hard decisions as a result.

Las Vegas Raiders

2026 cap space: $87.8 million

Top free agent: Eric Stokes

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: The Raiders have one of the NFL's best rushers -- for now, as Maxx Crosby is expected to request a trade. Tight end Brock Bowers and running back Ashton Jeanty are among the most promising young players at their positions. The team hired Seahawks offensive coordinator Klint Kubiak as its head coach, and it is in position to select Heisman Trophy-winning quarterback Fernando Mendoza with the No. 1 pick in April's draft.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The roster needs work at nearly every position, Crosby isn't likely to stick around, and coaches rarely win in their first seasons. Las Vegas was the league's worst team in 2025, and it has a ways to go.

Miami Dolphins

2026 cap space: minus-$27.5 million

Top free agent: Rasul Douglas

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: De'Von Achane is a premier tailback, Aaron Brewer has emerged as a top center, and Patrick Paul is a rising star at left tackle. The team won't lose much in free agency, which will allow it to allocate resources elsewhere.

Why it might take longer than hoped: The Dolphins would probably shun the idea of rebuilding publicly, but I'll embrace it for them. A new regime that will start over at quarterback and has plenty of roster holes might as well lean into a rebuild. The quarterback situation is a mess, with Tua Tagovailoa owed $54 million in guarantees and seemingly not in the team's plans. And the defense doesn't have enough high-level playmakers. The overall depth on that side of the ball is poor. Dolphins fans should have some patience.

New York Jets

2026 cap space: $58.6 million

Top free agent: Breece Hall

Projected 2026 draft picks: 8

The hope for a quick turnaround: The Jets' roster still has talent. There's also talent along the offensive line and at wide receiver, where Garrett Wilson should be healthy. New York can retain running back Hall via the franchise or transition tag. The front seven has enough ability to improve last season's 29th-ranked defense. And the front office is well positioned in GM Darren Mougey's second year, thanks to ample cap space and two picks in each of the first two rounds.

title: Pete Dougherty has answers on Packers leadership, NFL draft priorities - PackersNews.com

text:
GREEN BAY − Green Bay Press-Gazette and PackersNews columnist Pete Dougherty responds to reader questions on the hot topics of the Green Bay Packers' offseason.

Is winning big the Packers’ priority?

Gerry F: Hi Pete, thanks for taking questions. Do you think there is actual accountability on the Packers' coaching staff and front office? MLF [Matt LaFleur], Gute [Brian Gutekunst] and [Ed] Policy have been together for a long time and seem chummy to me. There seems like a lot of complacency about just making the playoffs, etc. Yes, they want to win more but not enough to make changes when they do not. Who holds them truly accountable? The Executive Committee? And do they do any more than rubber stamp management’s decisions? Seems like that to me. Sometimes I think the Packers suffer by not having an owner who demands accountability and will make hard decisions even if he likes the people involved.

Pete Dougherty: Hi everybody. Let’s dive right in.

A lot going on in this question. I’ll start with the last part, which is the most important in my opinion.

There are advantages and disadvantages to being the Packers. A disadvantage? They don’t have a multibillionaire owner who can dip into his pocket if need be. They can’t sell a percentage of the franchise to private equity (or minority owner) for a quick infusion of cash. They have to be more sensitive to public concerns because of their public ownership history.

But they have advantages as well, and one of their biggest is their ownership structure. The potential issues of complacency that you point to could just as easily happen at a franchise with a traditional owner as with the Packers. It depends solely on who’s in charge and setting the tone.

More to the point, other franchises are subject to the whim of an owner. That’s not a problem if it’s a good owner such as Robert Kraft or Jeffrey Lurie. But would you really want Jimmy Haslam running the Packers? Michael Bidwell? The Wilfs? The McCaskeys? Woody Johnson? Stephen Ross? Mike Brown? Jerry Jones?

Really, owners have less accountability than a Packers CEO. Owners can’t be fired. CEOs can. Would you really prefer a franchise subject to the moods of someone, as George Costanza said of George Steinbrenner, who fires people like it’s a bodily function?

It’s also worth pointing out that two of the best franchises in the league in recent years have been the Rams and Seahawks. They have two of the most hands-off, laissez faire owners in the league in Stan Kroenke and the Allens.

The Packers have their issues just like everyone else. Anyone who works in an office can tell you about all the pitfalls of human relations in trying to attain a goal.

But from everything I’ve witnessed the last 35 years covering this team and league, the Packers’ ownership structure is a net strength. It’s the reason Ted Thompson was able to draft Aaron Rodgers while Brett Favre still was playing, and Gutekunst was able to draft Jordan Love when Rodgers was still playing.

There was not an owner in the league who would have allowed the Rodgers pick with Favre still on the roster and playing good football. An owner would have had a personal relationship with Favre and insisted the GM draft someone who could help the team immediately, rather than someone who wouldn’t play as long as Favre was around. Probably the same with Love even though the Rodgers example was there for the world to see. Most owners in this league take a much shorter-term view than the Packers’ CEOs have for the past 35 years.

The Packers’ CEO has the wherewithal to do everything an owner can do as far as firing the coach and GM. That’s the ultimate accountability. Bob Harlan made a hard decision in firing Tom Braatz as GM before the ‘91 season even ended. Same for Mark Murphy with coach Mike McCarthy in ‘18.

The Packers’ recent CEOs have taken an approach similar to the Steelers where they’re willing to ride the NFL waves with their top football brass more than most teams. Stability is a big part of their identity.

It’s hard to argue it hasn’t succeeded for both franchises.

Feel free to ding the Packers for not winning more than two Super Bowl titles in the last 30 years with Rodgers and Favre at QB. That’s fair. But since 1992, the Packers have the second-best winning percentage in the NFL and the Steelers the third. New England, which had the most impactful player in league history for most of that time, is first.

Regardless, Policy knows what's riding on his decision to sign his GM and coach to significant extensions. He will feel the heat from all directions if it doesn’t produce improved results. One thing that goes with the job: The CEO is in charge of a public treasure, and there’s immense pressure to live up to the franchise's storied history.

I’m not dismissing your point. What you suggest about the Executive Committee is correct. When it comes to football it is a rubber stamp. Its role is ensure the franchise is being administered well. But again, there is at least theoretical accountability, because the Executive Committee can fire the CEO. There’s no firing an owner.

It’s also true the standards in Green Bay should be high. The Packers have plenty of resources, enough that they should be highly competitive. Everything is about the team, not the owner, and all profits go back into football. This franchise is set up for success.

It’s up to a combination of Policy and the Executive Committee to make sure the pieces are in place to uphold high standards in football. If you have a problem with Policy’s decisions this offseason, I would strongly argue your issue should be with him specifically, not with the ownership structure.

Al Mackie, Cinnaminson, NJ: Hi Pete, thanks for the chats. For me, this was probably one of the most disappointing seasons in the past decade (2021 ranks No. 1). I would have preferred to clean house after the season, but I can understand why Policy did not. After getting off to a good 2-0 start, and the trade for Micah Parsons, the Packers looked like the team to beat in the NFC. Then, even before all the injuries, they went back to a team that has a hard time playing four quarters of quality football. The coaching staff was slow to make in-game adjustments etc...etc… and even the games the Packers won were anything but impressive.

Then there is the roster-building process … the OL with all the changes and movement was a disaster and the CBs were almost as bad. Special teams were anything but, again! As a Packers fan and shareholder I hope Policy made the right decision, but what if he didn’t? Where do the Packers go if next year it’s the same old story or even worse? Cleaning house in January 2027 becomes almost impossible or very expensive.

Dougherty: Based on reporting from PackersNews and other outlets as well as my discussions with sources, it’s safe to assume Gutekunst and LaFleur now have either four or five years on their contracts.

That all but guarantees they’ll have at least two years, maybe three, to improve upon what they’ve done the last three seasons.

Now, things change fast in the NFL, and everything – everything – is fluid. So you never know what might happen in the next year. But doing that type of extension with his coach and GM was Policy’s way of saying that barring disaster, they'll get two to three years together to achieve more than they have.

Mike: Hi Pete! Thanks for taking our questions. I've read all the press/articles about Policy's explanation for retaining Gutey and LaFleur. I've read Gutey's comments about [Rich] Bisaccia, the “better” special teams, and certain players doing “great,” and it all leaves me with a very "meh" feeling.

Five straight losses are being chalked up to injuries? Nothing about draft misses, head coach decision making, second-half collapses across the board, special teams failures and repeated underperformance.

Is everything they say a smokescreen or do they really have their heads buried in the sand? Are we really an organization that is content with a wild-card playoff game with fingers crossed for better health? As a fan, it's really disappointing. If nothing else, I was hoping the organization would go back to a GM in charge of the head coach, and seeing some staff shakeup (Bisaccia out, new offensive line coach maybe). What kind of message is being sent about accountability in this organization?

Dougherty: Yes, much of what the GM and coach say publicly is a smokescreen, or an attempt to reveal as little as possible about what they’re thinking and what’s going on inside the building.

I also agree that changing the former structure and making the GM head of football would have been the better move. But I will say, I’m not in those offices every day as Policy is, and he apparently saw a chemistry between Gutekunst and LaFleur that he didn’t want to disrupt by making one of the boss of the other after they’d worked in the current structure the last seven years. It doesn’t mean Policy made the right call, but he is there.

I do have to say, though, three weeks after the season ended Gutekunst still appeared upset by the five straight losses to close the season and the inability to close out games. He referenced both multiple times, such as, when asked whether a specific player or two will be back next season, he said something to the effect of, “After five straight losses I’m not sure I can guarantee anyone will be back,” before answering the question.

Gutekunst’s other theme was the need to finish games better. But he’s rarely going to single out anyone by name for poor performance. Same with LaFleur. You see it leaguewide, coaches and GMs don’t do that publicly very often because it’s a good way to lose the locker room and staff. The question is, what are they doing behind the scenes?

As for Bisaccia, Gutekunst made it sound like he’ll definitely be back. I thought there was a better chance OL coach Luke Butkus would get let go after the offensive line struggles last season. But if he’d been fired I’d think it would have leaked out by now, so looks like that’s not happening either.

Have to admit I’m surprised by Butkus’ apparent return. The offensive line underachieved this season, and he’s responsible for that group. Maybe LaFleur exonerated him because of decisions Butkus might not have made. Was it his call to move Elgton Jenkins to center and Jordan Morgan all around? Or was that LaFleur and Adam Stenavich? Still, player development on the line hasn’t been great the last couple of years, and I thought Butkus would take the fall.

I’ll be interested to hear what LaFleur has to say about that the next time he meets with the media, whenever that is.

Who’s back, and who’s not?

George: Hi Pete, I understand that the response from the Packers when asked about a particular player is: “They are a Green Bay Packer and I expect them to be one next season.” Sounds like what every corporate company says before a major layoff with financial issues (over the cap).

So enough gumbo, who are the players whose cap hit is not represented by their performance? This does not mean they will be cut, but they could get a contact restructure, or just see how 2026 pans out. I think the Packers will cut and restructure as much as they can so they can sign free agents to fill holes at corner, offensive line and defensive line.

Dougherty: Yes, this is where we shouldn’t judge Gutekunst by what he says, only by what he does in the next month.

As ESPN.com’s Rob Demovsky has pointed out, Gutekunst in his season-ending press conference last year made it sound like Jaire Alexander might be back, and that was never going to happen. The year before he called Aaron Jones the heart and soul of the team, strongly suggesting he’d be back in 2024, but then Gutekunst turned around and cut Jones a few weeks later.

With that in mind, Gutekunst seemed to leave open the door to Rashan Gary’s return. But it still has to be a given Gary gets cut, right? Cutting him will save about $11M on the cap and $19.5M in cash. I just can’t imagine the Packers bringing him back, and I can’t imagine Gary being open to a pay cut if the Packers even offered.

Same for Elgton Jenkins, though Gutekunst wasn’t asked about him. Cutting Jenkins will save $19.5M on the cap and $17.6M in cash, and he appears to be in fast decline physically.

I still lean toward thinking Nate Hobbs will get cut too, though this is a much harder call. It won’t save much cap room ($838K) but it will save $9M in cash and future cap room. Hobbs is clearly better in the slot than on the outside, and with Javon Bullard as the starter in the slot it’s hard to see the Packers paying Hobbs $9M to be a backup cornerback.

Now, there’s always the chance new defensive coordinator Jonathan Gannon has plans for Hobbs, and maybe Gutekunst was telling the truth when he said he expected Hobbs back next season. It’s a borderline call. But I still lean toward thinking the Packers will cut him before the start of the league year in March.

The other close call is Aaron Banks. He played better at the end of the season as he got healthier, and my guess is the Packers will bring him back even though his play didn’t measure up to his cost. He’ll make $18.5M in salary and bonuses, though there’s a good chance the Packers will lower his cap number by turning his $9.5M roster bonus into a signing bonus.

The Packers almost surely will do other contract restructuring as well to create extra cap room, but like with Banks they don’t involve pay cuts. Xavier McKinney, for instance, has an $8.5M roster bonus that can be converted into a signing bonus to create room on the ‘25 cap.

Some praise for Brian Gutekunst

David Lang: People give Gutey all sorts of grief about his draft record. Sure he has some misses, but I feel he saved the Packers from complete disaster with his pick of Love in 2020, and not just because Love is a top 10 if not elite QB. Just play that draft out and imagine they rode with Rodgers long term. Since then the only QBs drafted that would have been around when they picked that weren't completely terrible are Jalen Hurts, Malik Willis and Brock Purdy. I doubt they would have gone for any of those guys except Willis.

Imagine we had a 41-year-old Rodgers with no backup last season. No way they trade two firsts for Micah Parsons. We would be stuck tanking in ‘26 and ‘27 in hopes of getting a franchise QB. Quibble all you want about a pick they missed on in R2 or R3, without the extremely prescient pick of Love we would be walking into at least three to four years of dumpster-fire football right now.

Dougherty: You’re not going anywhere in this league without a good quarterback, and you usually have to draft one to find one. Sam Darnold is very much the exception, not the rule.

Gutekunst achieved a rare feat overseeing the seamless transition from a Hall of Fame quarterback to Love. It was a risky pick that drew loads of criticism. But Gutekunst got the evaluation right. Yes, it now needs to translate into more than early-round exits from the playoffs, but drafting Love has given the Packers a chance.

The quarterback carousel is an awful place to be in the NFL, and the Packers have avoided it for 35 years and counting. I can’t think of another team that’s had that long a stretch of good QB play.

Was great defense the lesson of the Super Bowl?

Jesse: I thought it was interesting that neither quarterback playing in the Super Bowl had a rating over 80 in the game. Does that tell you teams like the Packers need to draft heavily in favor of defense? I was thinking with backup offensive linemen Travis Glover and John Williams on the shelf this year, the only offensive player we might draft is a running back in a late round. Your thoughts?

Dougherty: I try not to draw too many conclusions from any Super Bowl, because things usually change from year to year, and there are different ways to get to the promised land.

When the Rams won the SB with an “eff-them picks” roster, all the talk was that teams should trade picks for proven stars. But that quickly fell out of favor as the Rams quickly fell off. Last year Howie Roseman was the toast of the NFL for building the Eagles’ dominant offensive and defensive lines. This year, they exited in the wild-card round of the playoffs. Kansas City has won three SBs in recent years with great offense (and a great QB).

There’s no one blueprint for winning a championship.

You don’t have to be as good as Seattle was on defense this season to win a title, but it’s hard not to agree the Packers need to get better on that side of the ball. Seven of Gutekunst’s 10 first-round picks (in eight drafts) have come on defense, and yet that side of the ball is still lagging.

Cornerback and defensive tackle are their greatest needs in my mind – I have to think most Packers observers would agree. I think they need to add two significant players at each spot via a combination of free agency and the draft.

But I think they’ll have to draft at more than RB on offense. Glover and Williams are too unproven to count on, so they need to draft at least one OL and probably two. Backup QB will be a big need as well.

Phil Lewis: Why has GB been so bad at putting together strong defensive units over say the last 25 years? Seattle just won another Bowl with its D, a group dominant like Legion of Boom but different personnel.

Dougherty: Probably one of two things, or a combination.

The Seahawks built those great defenses led by a defensive head coach – Pete Carroll with the Legion of Boom, now Mike Macdonald with The Dark Side. Having a defensive-minded coach matters, both in the way the team approaches player acquisition and its mindset and expertise on defense.

It also might be that Seahawks GM John Schneider is better than his peers at identifying defensive talent.

Jeff P: Are the Packers going to stop being pretenders and finally spend on much needed veteran help to plug the gaping holes on defense across the board? Oh, and let's not forget the ol’ offensive line.

Dougherty: Not sure if this is what you’re talking about, but I’ve been wondering lately if it’s a must in today’s NFL to sign a couple of stopgap veterans even if they’re on the older side to plug a hole or two in the roster. It’s just too hard to do it with the draft alone, and too expensive to constantly add big-ticket free agents.

Generally, signing older veterans (29, 30, 31) isn’t a great way to do business. That’s an age when a lot of players really start falling off, and when it happens, it usually happens fast. Like, in one offseason. It’s risky to invest much money, and if you do it a lot, your team can get old in a hurry.

That’s definitely not the way Thompson or Gutekunst have done it, so it also would be a big departure for the Packers.

But the Packers can’t count on upgrading their need positions only through the draft, and even if they spend big on a free agent or two, there will be other holes. I wonder if signing a few cheaper, more bargain-type veterans might be a way to go with the idea that you’re looking for only one more good year out of them.

Teams have two competing agendas in that way. One is, they want to get young guys on the field who have a chance to improve as the season goes on. Playing older, declining players in front of them only gets in the way of that improvement.

But if they have a good team, sometimes modest but ready-made upgrades at a position or two can make a difference. I do wonder if there’s a place for a couple of signings like that rather than only going after 25- or 26-year-olds in free agency, as Gutekunst generally has done.

What does Matt LaFleur’s offense hang its hat on?

Nabob: Hey Pete. Be head coach for the day. You've been around the block (as have I) to witness NFL from Lombardi era to current day. Hard to believe it's the same game! Offensive coaches now frequently pander to cerebral egos and focus on “creating matchups.” Lombardi meanwhile was about blocking, tackling and execution. The power sweep was going to be run regardless, and unstoppable due to precise, ingrained execution. [Mike] Holmgren hung his hat on screens.

MLF has zero unstoppable “identity” plays. Mistake? Similar observation, Jordy Nelson was unstoppable with sideline back-shoulder fades. Christian Watson certainly has deep-threat speed and Love the arm talent to throw the back shoulder, yet rarely does MLF implement it. That seems to be an unstoppable matchup opportunity that is being underutilized. Personally, I'd like to see MLF have one or two signature plays they run a half dozen times a game with a “just try to stop it” mentality.

Dougherty: The gist of the LaFleur offense, which is from the Kyle Shanahan tree, is running the ball well and then having everything else play off that. Everything up to and just past the snap looks the same to the defense, run or pass. The biggest problem the Packers had offensively last season is they didn’t run the ball well. That’s where they lacked identity at times. Maybe that’s what you’re talking about.

But your question also mentions Lombardi, and it caught my eye because it just so happens I’ve talked a lot about him with the Packers’ historian, Cliff Christl, over the years. Though Lombardi is often caricatured as a one-note martinet, Christl says one of Lombardi’s great strengths was game planning.

For instance, Zeke Bratkwoski, who played or coached for 10 organizations in the NFL from 1954 through 1996, called Lombardi the best game planner he ever saw.

Bratkowski said Lombardi was exceptional at identifying weak points in a defense and favorable matchups for his offense, and then exploiting them. It’s not like that approach started with Bill Belichick. So yeah, Lombardi’s execution on the sweep was great. But his success was based on a lot more than that. He was a master game planner, too.

To your main point, coaches always have to walk that line between concentrating on a few things their team does well, and having the versatility to adjust based on the matchup in any given week. LaFleur has had another season to learn about Love. One thing we know, Love throws the long out pattern as well as any QB in the league. Maybe we’ll see more of that in ‘26.

Draft priorities

Mark: I believe the top three needs for next year are the offensive and defensive lines and cornerback. How would you prioritize these three and am I missing something that should be ahead of these three areas?

Dougherty: I’d put CB and DT as the highest priorities. Not sure I can say one is a greater need than the other.

Cornerback is a premium position, and we saw in the Super Bowl how much it helps to have a high-end cornerback (Christian Gonzalez on New England, and Devon Witherspoon for the Seahawks). They were maybe the two best players on the field

Not that the Packers need a cornerback of their quality to upgrade.

On the other hand, the game starts up front, and strong defensive tackle play seems more important than ever for stopping the run with only six in the box and pressuring the passer up the middle.

As I said, if I were the Packers I’d be looking to add two significant players at each spot from some combination of free agency and the draft. Don’t know if Gutekunst sees it that way too. He might see the need for only one at each.

How to know if the Micah Parsons trade was good?

Schenectedy Paul: Many “analysts” have proclaimed the Parsons trade a good/great deal for the Pack. I personally don’t think Parsons puts the Pack into Super Bowl contention territory. What criteria would you use to, say four years from now, evaluate whether the trade was good or bad for the Pack?

Dougherty: I can’t say they have to win or even get to the Super Bowl to judge the trade a good one.

The critierion I’ll use is, did he make the defense significantly better? The answer last season was a resounding yes. I have to admit I was a little astonished by how impactful he was. There are a lot of ways to measure it, but one simple one is the Packers were 9-3-1 in games he finished and 0-5 in the games he didn’t, including the game at Denver when he sustained the torn ACL.

Did he make them a SB contender? I thought he did, though Seattle showed it was the best team in the league. But even if you don’t think he quite got them over that hump, it’s hard to argue they’re not a lot closer with him.

The question now is how much the ACL injury will affect him going forward. It’s an injury that can diminish explosive players. He might never be the same. On the other hand, Parsons is such an exceptional athlete it might not affect him as much as it has others.

Either way, I’ll judge the trade by how impactful he is on games. If he can stay in the neighborhood of where he was before the injury – no guarantee on that – then it will be a great trade.

Joe Cool: As always, thanks for the chat, Pete. I have a simple question (but maybe the answer isn't simple): Is Micah Parsons expected to be ready for the start of next season, or is his injury one that carries over into the regular season like Christian Watson's did?

Dougherty: Parsons said he’s aiming to return by Week 3 or 4 next season. That would have him back about 9½ to 10 months after his injury, which he sustained Dec. 14.

The Packers often make players wait 10 months before they can return to game action. But Watson returned to game play 9¾ months after his torn ACL. Parsons is aiming to do the same. If he does it, he’ll still miss two or three games.

Did Gutekunst blow it with T.J. Slaton?

Carl Gerbschmidt, Elk Mound, WI: How badly does Gutey regret not re-signing Slaton?

Dougherty: During and shortly after the season I answered that with, “a lot.” Turns out, maybe not so much.

The Bengals reportedly are considering cutting Slaton after one season to save about $6.6M in cap room. They only owe him $6.1M this season, so if they cut him it will mean they consider the price excessive.

If, in fact, the Bengals cut him, it means I was wrong, and Gutekunst made the right call letting him walk.

What current players will get better?

Kevin: Hi Pete. The Packers need improvement from within the current roster to compete next year. Who do you think are the best candidates to make a jump next year? Any practice squad or futures guys to watch?

Dougherty: Can’t say any of the practice-squad players stands out as a sleeper.

But there are plenty of young players who could improve in the offseason.

The Packers, in fact, need Jordan Morgan to make a nice jump in Year 3, because he’s going to be protecting Love’s blind side.

Almost the entire ‘25 draft class is a candidate as well.

The best bet for a big jump is of course Matthew Golden. He flashed a lot of talent in training camp, and then again in the playoff loss at Chicago.

I’ll be curious to see how Savion Williams does also. He was very raw as a receiver coming into the league this year, so he might have a more gradual growth curve. But he needs to become a real receiver rather than being mostly a gadget/niche guy.

Anthony Belton is well positioned to make a big jump. He ended up playing a lot at right guard, so he now knows the lay of the land as an NFL guard. With a full offseason to work at his new position he could improve quickly.

Barryn Sorrell and Collin Oliver are candidates too. I’m especially interested to see how Oliver does, because he’s a pass rusher. He might end up being too small to be a good NFL rusher, but he is an interesting prospect after missing almost all of last season because of a hamstring injury.

How does scouting work?

Paddy: My questions are about the scouting staff. How many are there? Who do they directly report to? Do they have designated regions to cover? Do they look at D2 or D3 schools? Finally, do they recommend who to bring in for pre-draft visits?

Dougherty: The Packers have 21 scouts on staff, including Gutekunst. They lost Jon-Eric Sullivan to Miami’s GM position but just this week reportedly hired a new scout, former Auburn University GM Will Redmond.

It’s unclear what Redmond’s role will be, though it likely will skew more toward a lower-level position.

The Packers’ highest-ranking executives under Gutekunst have dual pro/college duties and overall staff responsibilities: Milt Hendrickson is director-football operations, and John Wojciechowski is director of player personnel. Lee Gissendaner is player personnel executive and presumably a candidate for promotion now that Sullivan has left.

They have a director of pro personnel (Richmond Williams), and three others have the title “pro scout.”

They have 11 college scouts. Matt Malaspina is director of college scouting, and longtime college scout Sam Seale is listed as one of two national college scouts. Most of the remaining eight are responsible for specific geographic areas of the country, though the Packers no longer list the area of responsibility for each scout.

Yes, the college scouts look at DII and DIII players. Off the top of my head the last DII player I can remember the Packers drafting was offensive lineman Allen Barbre (Missouri Southern State) in the fourth round in 2007.

Gutekunst has final say over the 30 pre-draft visits. I don’t know the process for determining those players, though. They bring guys in for a variety of reasons. With some, it’s for a medical recheck. With others, it’s to get to know them better if there are character questions. With others it’s to get them on the grease board to test their knowledge and find out what kind of learners they are.

I wouldn’t be surprised if they occasionally bring in someone to make other teams think they’re more interested in a highly ranked player than they really are (the visits have to be reported to the league and are available to all teams).

Will Super Bowl change game management in NFL?

Steven Rosenbaum: Pete – with the Seahawks kicking numerous field goals and winning, do you think that’ll inspire a coach to become extra conservative and kick everything moving forward? Basically a shift entirely away from the Dan Campbell methodology.

Dougherty: No, I don’t expect a shift away from the NFL trend of going for fourth downs.

I think the field goals in the Super Bowl were a function of that game with those opponents. Seattle was shutting down New England, and I’m sure at a certain point in the first half Macdonald saw the Patriots were going to have a tough time scoring, so he just needed to accrue points, and field goals were fine. You could see it in Seattle’s offensive play calling in the red zone, too. The Seahawks weren’t going to try risky throws to get in the end zone. They just wanted points.

But to play that way you have to have extreme confidence in your defense. McDonald had the best defense in the league, and Drake Maye was struggling (in part because of his youth, and I’m sure in part because of the injury to his throwing arm). Not many teams could have that kind of confidence in their defense. If he were playing the Bills, Macdonald probably would have had a different mindset.
